Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because I'm 29 and juggling a lot more than just school. I tried to carve out time on weekends and a couple evenings during the week, but it rarely went according to plan. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often set up practice sessions where I could work through problems physically, writing things out and testing concepts. The 13 hours I managed to study each week felt like a constant battle to fit in around everything else in my life.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life and when assignments were due. I'd have weeks where I was pretty consistent, then other weeks where I barely touched anything until a deadline loomed. Being older and having responsibilities outside of school meant I couldn't always stick to a neat schedule. Deadlines definitely dictated my rhythm more than I would have liked.
Q3: Usually it was an assignment deadline that got me to log in, or sometimes I'd remember I needed to check if there were announcements I'd missed. I wasn't someone who browsed the LMS casually—I went in with a purpose and got out. Sometimes a peer would mention something from class and I'd realize I needed to catch up on materials I'd missed due to my attendance issues.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a natural part of my routine. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more regularly, especially when I was trying to catch up after missing classes. But it always felt like a chore rather than something automatic. I think because I learn better by doing things physically, sitting at a computer to engage with course materials just didn't stick as a habit for me.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful because I could pause and rewind when I needed to work through something hands-on alongside them. I watched quite a bit of video content—nearly five hours worth—because that was how I could compensate for my poor attendance. Any resources that let me practice and apply concepts rather than just read about them felt more valuable to my learning style.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—I think I only posted once the whole course. They felt performative to me, like I was just posting for the sake of posting rather than actually learning anything. I also ignored a lot of the supplementary readings because I didn't have time to get through everything, so I had to prioritize what would directly help with assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what I needed to complete or understand for an assignment. I also valued anything that let me engage physically—like practice problems I could work through on paper or simulations I could manipulate. If something felt like busywork or just clicking through screens, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With my limited study time and everything else going on, I had to be ruthless about what I spent energy on. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones rarely made the cut unless a peer specifically recommended them. It's not that I didn't see their value—I just had to make hard choices about time.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then print them out so I could annotate by hand and spread everything out on my table. I also used textbooks from the library and YouTube videos to supplement what I was learning. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to physically interact with materials, not just scroll through them on a screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd be at my kitchen table with printed notes, working through problems by hand for hours, and none of that would show up anywhere in the system. I'd also meet up with peers from the course—I had quite a few interactions with classmates—and we'd study together in person, which was incredibly helpful but invisible to the LMS. My real learning often happened away from the computer.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or watch a video once to get the gist, then continue working with that material offline for days. I'd print things, make flashcards, or recreate diagrams by hand. The LMS would only show that initial access, but the real engagement came after, when I was physically working with the content in ways that suited how I learn.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when my attendance dropped and I felt like I was falling behind. Some personal stuff was weighing on me—I've been feeling pretty sad lately—and it made it hard to muster the energy to engage. Missing classes created this snowball effect where catching up felt overwhelming, even though I kept pushing through my assignments.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor. Being 29 and managing life responsibilities while studying meant I was often exhausted by the time I sat down to do coursework. When I was tired or stressed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—check what was due, download what I needed, and log off. The competing responsibilities definitely shaped how and when I could engage with the platform.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I'd log in and scroll through materials but my mind was elsewhere. I'd watch videos without really absorbing anything because I was too tired or preoccupied. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning. I'd often have to go back later and re-engage with the same content when I was in a better headspace.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation more than I should have, which is probably why my exam score was so low despite doing well on assignments. I think I avoided it because exams feel so high-stakes and anxiety-inducing, and I kept telling myself I'd have time later. The assignments felt more manageable—concrete tasks I could complete—while studying for exams felt nebulous and overwhelming.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation to log in, download materials, and work intensively. My assignment completion rate was high because I always managed to pull through when it mattered, but my LMS activity probably spiked dramatically around due dates rather than being spread evenly throughout the course.
Q17: Feedback on assignments actually motivated me to keep going, especially when I did well. Seeing that my assignment scores were solid gave me confidence that I understood the material, even when other things weren't going great. But feedback on exams was harder to process—seeing that low score was discouraging, though I tried to use it as information about what I needed to work on differently.
Q18: The course structure with regular assignments worked well for me because it gave me consistent checkpoints to work toward. But the heavy weighting on exams hurt me badly since I struggle with that format. Having more flexibility in how we could demonstrate learning would have helped someone like me who does well with applied work but freezes up during timed tests.
Q19: The video content being available to watch anytime made engagement easier since I could fit it around my schedule. But the discussion requirements felt forced and didn't match how I naturally learn or connect with peers. I learn better through direct interaction with people, like the peer support I've been getting, rather than typing posts into a forum that feels artificial.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to engage but struggling to produce quality work, maybe due to confusion or personal challenges. They might be logging in, watching videos, reading materials, but can't translate that into completed assignments because something's blocking them. High activity without output might signal someone who needs help but doesn't know how to ask for it.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me in some ways. My LMS activity was relatively low, but I completed almost all my assignments and did well on them because so much of my real studying happened offline. Someone looking at my login frequency might think I wasn't trying, but I was putting in serious work—just not in ways the system could see. The LMS only captures one dimension of engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, whether you meet deadlines. But they miss the quality of engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't tell if you were really learning when you watched that video or just had it playing in the background. And they definitely can't capture the emotional journey—the stress, the sadness, the determination to keep going despite setbacks.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious about understanding something deeply, when you discuss ideas with peers and find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time. For me, engagement is physical too—writing, drawing diagrams, explaining things out loud. It's an active state of mind, not just a set of logged behaviors.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the peer support I received helped me stay resilient when things got hard. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying with printed materials spread across my table, or the conversations with classmates that helped concepts click. It also can't show that despite failing the course, I learned a lot and I'm not giving up. My engagement was real, even if the data doesn't fully reflect it.
